summary: High‑energy Spring ’87 set with blazing Jerry solos & tight drums
blurb: The show roars from the opening Alabama Getaway into a second‑set surge featuring Touch of Grey, Samson & Delilah and a soaring Uncle John's Band → Morning Dew. Jerry is on fire, Brent’s Leslie‑speaker Rooster solo shines, and the twin drummers drive an unrelenting groove. Recommended for anyone wanting a quintessential late‑’80s Dead experience.
review: On March 27 1987 at the Hartford Civic Center the Grateful Dead delivered one of those high‑octane spring tours that still sparks conversation among Deadheads. The first set laid down a solid groove, but it was the second set that truly ignited the night – Touch of Grey erupted into a fist‑raising anthem, Samson & Delilah unfolded with a thunderous drum intro, and the band rode an unbroken wave through Cumberland Blues, Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World and a blistering Uncle John's Band → Morning Dew. Jerry Garcia’s guitar was “on fire,” delivering soaring solos that many reviewers called ablaze, while Brent Mydland’s Leslie‑speaker Rooster solo added a head‑blowing texture. Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann kept the rhythm tight and intense, and Phil Lesh’s bass dropped deep groove bombs throughout. The encore’s Johnny B. Goode capped an unforgettable evening, making this show a standout entry in the 1987 spring run.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Six out of eight recordings praise the performance as high‑energy, with consistent mentions of Jerry ‘on fire’, standout Touch of Grey and Samson jams, tight drumming and brilliant Brent keyboard moments; no significant negative commentary on playing quality.

best_recording.reason: Superb 24‑bit matrix capture with crisp bass, clear drums and a balanced crowd mix that showcases the high‑energy performance and all standout moments.

key_highlights: Blazing Jerry solos across Touch of Grey, Samson & Delilah and Morning Dew
Brent Mydland’s Leslie‑speaker Rooster solo adds head‑blowing texture
Twin drums pound with intensity, driving the entire second set
Uncle John's Band → Morning Dew sequence ranks among late‑’80s classics

band_performance.Jerry: On fire all night, delivering soaring, blistering solos on Touch of Grey, Samson & Delilah and the climactic Morning Dew.
band_performance.Phil: Lays down deep groove bombs; his bass work is fast, heavy and especially prominent during the Estimated Prophet/AM Dew bridge.
band_performance.Bob: Goes berserk on guitar, providing strong rhythm chops and vocal energy throughout the set.
band_performance.Brent: Leslie‑speaker Rooster solo shines; his keyboard textures are jazzy, layered and add gospel‑like color to the jams.
band_performance.Mickey: Drumming is tight, intense and drives the high‑energy flow of both sets.
band_performance.Billy: Pairs with Mickey for a pounding, straight‑ahead rhythm that fuels the show's relentless momentum.
